name: River Strand
bio: River Strand works in the space between the botanical and the surreal, weaving intricate linework with Art Nouveau-inspired forms. Their gallery-quality paintings layer organic motifs with muted pastel palettes that reward sustained attention.
artistStatement: I seek to capture the in-between—the moment before form settles, where possibility still breathes.

style.palette: Muted pastels, ivory, sage, dusty rose

name: Ren Cross
bio: Ren Cross brings a cinematic darkness to portraiture, painting uncanny figures that hover between folk tale and dream. Deeply influenced by classic illustration, their work transforms the antique into something quietly unsettling.
artistStatement: Every face holds a story older than the person wearing it.

name: Elara Nakamura
bio: Elara Nakamura merges Japanese woodblock tradition with Art Nouveau sinuousness, producing luminous figures that feel both ancient and contemporary. Her graceful compositions have drawn collectors from across the Pacific Rim.
artistStatement: Beauty is a discipline—every line must earn its place.

style.medium: Ink and wood stain on birch panel
style.subjects: Female figures with floral and natural motifs

name: Nova Ash
bio: Nova Ash paints softly luminous dreamscapes where figures dissolve into forests and starfields. Her work has a quality of remembered childhood wonder—approachable yet quietly melancholic.
artistStatement: I paint the places I go when I close my eyes.

name: Elara Chen
bio: Elara Chen fuses street art energy with fine art craft, building high-contrast works that carry the visual weight of propaganda posters and the emotional depth of protest art. Her bold compositions demand attention.
artistStatement: Art without urgency is decoration. I want my work to mean something.

name: Vera Voss
bio: Vera Voss paints luminous figurative works of extraordinary technical refinement—gilded surfaces, cascading fabric, and translucent glazes that recall Old Master technique in a contemporary context. Her pieces are among the most sought-after in the collection.
artistStatement: Light is the subject. Everything else is just its occasion.

style.medium: Oil and gold leaf on panel

name: Leo Vale
bio: Leo Vale paints ephemeral figures suspended between waking and dreaming — translucent forms dissolving into washes of colour, faces half-formed, expressions caught in the liminal moment before recognition. The work is tender and technically precise in equal measure.
artistStatement: I am trying to paint the feeling of a word on the tip of your tongue.

name: Mae Ryden
bio: Mae Ryden paints in the unsettling space between a child's music box and something older and stranger underneath. Working in small-format oil panels, she builds tableaux where wide-eyed porcelain-skinned figures share space with carnival automata, candy ephemera, and Victorian keepsakes rendered with obsessive precision. Her work has accumulated a devoted collector base drawn to its quality of beautiful wrongness — too meticulous to be naive, too sweet to be a warning.
artistStatement: I paint the way a music box sounds — innocent on the surface, mechanical underneath, and somehow both at once.

style.palette: Candy pink, porcelain white, crimson, pale gold
style.subjects: Pop surrealist figures with carnival and Victorian imagery

name: Fenna Haas
bio: Fenna Haas paints from a countryside studio in the Netherlands, where the garden outside her window is maintained with a care that suggests she finds animals more interesting than people. Her small-format paintings give rodents, birds, and foxes the compositional dignity of Renaissance portraiture — muted umber and olive grounds, delicate linework, an expression that carries the full weight of human emotional life without once trying to. Collectors describe the work as quietly devastating.
artistStatement: An animal never poses. That is what makes them harder to paint and more honest to look at.

style.medium: Oil and ink on wood panel
style.subjects: Anthropomorphic creature portraits with folk art dignity

name: Hiro Tomo
bio: Hiro Tomo trained under traditional Japanese irezumi masters in Osaka before developing a gallery practice that carries the discipline and sacred iconography of body art onto fine canvas. His primary subject is the cat — rendered with the compositional reverence given to dragons and guardian deities in classical Japanese tattooing. Koi, chrysanthemums, and waves orbit his subjects like mythological attributes. The work sits at a rare intersection of craft tradition and contemporary art market, attracting collectors from both worlds.
artistStatement: The tattoo artist works on skin that breathes and ages. I work on canvas that outlasts us both. The cat, of course, outlasts everyone.

style.palette: Red, sumi black, white, chrysanthemum gold
style.subjects: Cats and sacred animals in Japanese tattoo iconography

name: Bea Gomez
bio: Bea Gomez works from a small apartment in Barcelona where the objects on her windowsill have been arranged with too much care for someone who claims not to be precious about things. Her clean-line illustrations practice a kind of radical attention — the unremarkable given enough space and clarity that you see it properly for the first time. A cup of tea, a dog in a doorway, a single sock mid-air. Her palette runs from warm sage to dusty coral, with the occasional shock of yellow that makes you briefly, inexplicably happy.
artistStatement: I am interested in the things you have stopped noticing. I am trying to return them to you.

style.medium: Digital illustration printed on archival paper
style.palette: Sage, dusty coral, warm cream, occasional yellow
style.subjects: Everyday domestic objects rendered with poetic attention

name: Rave Zola
bio: Rave Zola's paintings arrive looking like they belong in a museum. Then you notice what they are actually saying. Working in the tradition of figurative oil painting, Zola applies Old Master technique — the lighting is Caravaggio, the compositional authority is Velázquez — to contemporary subjects that have something urgent to communicate. The gallery world has taken notice. Several major collections have acquired pieces without quite knowing how to categorise them, which is exactly where Zola wants to be.
artistStatement: Great painting technique is a Trojan horse. You let it through the gates before you realise what is inside.

style.palette: Deep chiaroscuro, raw umber, scarlet, aged white
style.subjects: Classical figurative composition with contemporary disruption

name: Ren Vale
bio: Ren Vale is an artist whose work draws on Jed Henry-inspired contemporary art with distinctive visual style and technique. Their pieces explore the boundaries between tradition and contemporary expression, creating compositions that invite contemplation and connection.
artistStatement: I seek to capture the in-between—the moment before form settles, where possibility still breathes.

style.subjects: Jed Henry-inspired contemporary art with distinctive visual style and technique

name: Kai Vale
bio: Kai Vale is an artist whose work draws on mid-century modern illustration, retro cocktail culture, geometric stylized figures, bold saturated colors, tiki and space-age aesthetics. Their pieces explore the boundaries between tradition and contemporary expression, creating compositions that invite contemplation and connection.
artistStatement: I seek to capture the in-between—the moment before form settles, where possibility still breathes.

style.subjects: mid-century modern illustration, retro cocktail culture, geometric stylized figures, bold saturated colors, tiki and space-age aesthetics
